problemTitle: Delete Nodes From Linked List Present in Array

sources.code:
# Time:  O(n)
# Space: O(m)

# hash table, linked list
class Solution(object):
    def modifiedList(self, nums, head):
        """
        :type nums: List[int]
        :type head: Optional[ListNode]
        :rtype: Optional[ListNode]
        """
        lookup = set(nums)
        curr = dummy = ListNode(0, head)
        while curr.next:
            if curr.next.val not in lookup:
                curr = curr.next
            else:
                curr.next = curr.next.next
        return dummy.next
sources.originalCode:
# Time:  O(n)
# Space: O(m)

# hash table, linked list
class Solution(object):
    def modifiedList(self, nums, head):
        """
        :type nums: List[int]
        :type head: Optional[ListNode]
        :rtype: Optional[ListNode]
        """
        lookup = set(nums)
        curr = dummy = ListNode(0, head)
        while curr.next:
            if curr.next.val not in lookup:
                curr = curr.next
            else:
                curr.next = curr.next.next
        return dummy.next
sources.reviewedCode:
# Time:  O(n)
# Space: O(m)

# hash table, linked list
# [Reader note] Entry point
# Start from the core routine and keep this mental model in view: Solve membership first, then make the list walk a calm delete-or-keep routine.
class Solution(object):
    def modifiedList(self, nums, head):
        """
        :type nums: List[int]
        # [Reader note] State setup
        # This setup stage creates the state needed for the invariant or partial result: Sentinel-based deletion, membership preprocessing, and keeping the traversal logic simpler than the problem story suggests.
        :type head: Optional[ListNode]
        :rtype: Optional[ListNode]
        """
        lookup = set(nums)
        curr = dummy = ListNode(0, head)
        # [Reader note] Main pass
        # This is the main sweep where the implementation earns its efficiency instead of restarting work from scratch.
        while curr.next:
            # [Reader note] Decision point
            # Pay attention here: The data-structure choice is straightforward; the subtle part is deleting cleanly at the head and through runs of removable nodes.
            if curr.next.val not in lookup:
                curr = curr.next
            else:
                curr.next = curr.next.next
        # [Reader note] Return
        # The final return exposes the result after the traversal and state updates have settled.
        return dummy.next

sources.localPath: doocs_leetcode/solution/3200-3299/3217.Delete Nodes From Linked List Present in Array/Solution.py

sources.reviewedCode:
# Definition for singly-linked list.
# class ListNode:
# [Reader note] Entry point
# Start from the core routine and keep this mental model in view: Solve membership first, then make the list walk a calm delete-or-keep routine.
#     def __init__(self, val=0, next=None):
# [Reader note] State setup
# This setup stage creates the state needed for the invariant or partial result: Sentinel-based deletion, membership preprocessing, and keeping the traversal logic simpler than the problem story suggests.
#         self.val = val
#         self.next = next
class Solution:
    def modifiedList(
        self, nums: List[int], head: Optional[ListNode]
    ) -> Optional[ListNode]:
        s = set(nums)
        pre = dummy = ListNode(next=head)
        # [Reader note] Main pass
        # This is the main sweep where the implementation earns its efficiency instead of restarting work from scratch.
        while pre.next:
            # [Reader note] Decision point
            # Pay attention here: The data-structure choice is straightforward; the subtle part is deleting cleanly at the head and through runs of removable nodes.
            if pre.next.val in s:
                pre.next = pre.next.next
            else:
                pre = pre.next
        # [Reader note] Return
        # The final return exposes the result after the traversal and state updates have settled.
        return dummy.next
